Pure Functions

react.gg
Pure Functions

It may seem basic, but as a software developer, your primary job is to maximize the predictability of your program.

Think about it. The root cause of any bug you've ever experienced was because the expectations of the developer diverged from the reality of the program – i.e. the program was unpredictable.

Pizza cart0

Assuming this is true, it's not unreasonable to think that we should spend at least some time thinking of how we can make our programs more predictable. After all, a world in which our applications are entirely predictable is also a world in which bugs cease to exist.

To better figure this out, I think it would be beneficial to narrow our scope. Make apps more predictable is too broad of a goal to be of any practical use. Instead, I think it's safe to assume that we can make our apps more predictable by making the individual pieces that make up our app more predictable – and what are those pieces? Usually they're functions.

A function is a process which takes some input, an argument, and calculates some output, a return value. Functions, by definition, are simple. The problem, as you're well aware, is nothing screws up the simplicity of a definition quite like the complexity of reality.

In reality, functions are often the junk drawer of your application. What often starts off as a simple, composable function, can, if you're not careful, transform into an unpredictable mess as your application grows.

In the real world, we can solve these sort of "eventually chaotic" scenarios by having a strict set of rules and then diligently adhering to them. What if we took the same approach here? Are there a set of rules we can make to keep our functions from becoming unpredictable over time?

To better answer that, let me ask a different question – what makes a function unpredictable? If we can answer this question, then we can create a set of rules to avoid those scenarios.

In my opinion, there are two big offenders – side effects and inconsistent outputs.

Side Effects

Earlier we defined a function as a process which takes some input, an argument, and calculates some output, a return value.

Any time a function does anything other than this, whether through relying on state other than the input value it receives or creating an observable change to the program itself, it's said to have a side effect.

Take this simple function.

function addTodo(todo) {
  todos.push(todo);
}

It both relies on state other than the input value it receives, and it creates an observable change to the program itself (by mutating todos) – therefore, it has a side effect.

What about this function?

function getGithubProfile(username) {
  return fetch(
    `https://api.github.com/users/${username}`
  ).then((res) => res.json());
}

It doesn't create an observable change to the program, but it does rely on state other than the input value it receives (state from an external API still counts). Therefore, this function has a side effect.

Now what about this one?

function updateDocumentTitle(title) {
  document.title = title;
}

You probably get the idea by now. We're not relying on state other than the input value it receives, but we are creating an observable change to the program itself. Therefore, another side effect.

So why are side effects bad? Well, they're not bad – they're just unpredictable.

One problem is, because they depend on the context outside of their local environments, they're contextual to the current state of the application. If the state of the application changes, they'll behave differently.

Take this function for example.

function calculateFinalPrice (price, qty) {
  const total = price * qty
  return total * (1 + TAX_RATE)
}

This function will only work in a program where TAX_RATE is both a variable and that variable is a decimal. If TAX_RATE gets mutated, or worse, deleted, calculateFinalPrice will behave differently – which by definition makes it unpredictable.

And with that, we have our first rule.

Rule #1

No Side Effects

Inconsistent Outputs

The next culprit in making functions unpredictable are inconsistent (or a lack of) outputs.

If a function doesn't have a return value, it's unpredictable.

thisIsUnpredictable()
soIsThis()

In this scenario, you can assume the function is either doing nothing (unlikely), or it has a side effect (breaking Rule #1).

Similarly, if a function, given the same input, doesn't consistently return the same output, it's also unpredictable.

const friends = ["Ben", "Lynn", "Alex"]


friends.splice(0, 1) // ["Ben"]
friends.splice(0, 1) // ["Lynn"]
friends.splice(0, 1) // ["Alex"]

Given the same input (0 and 1), splice returns a different output each time – making it unpredictable.

🍎

For Comparison

Unlike .splice, .slice is a similar array method that would be considered predictable.

const friends = ["Ben", "Lynn", "Alex"]


friends.slice(0, 1) // ["Ben"]
friends.slice(0, 1) // ["Ben"]
friends.slice(0, 1) // ["Ben"]

Notice .slice, given the same input, always returns the same output. It also doesn't mutate the original array (no side effects), so it passes both our rules and is considered predictable.

There are a variety of built-in methods you probably use regularly that are unpredictable for this exact reason.

Math.random() // 0.9511513628376609
Math.random() // 0.6547771399705873


Date.now() // 1665072516384
Date.now() // 1665072525093


const friends = ["Ben", "Lynn", "Alex"]
friends.push("Jeff") // 4
friends.push("Jeff") // 5

And it's not just built in methods that have this problem. Take the function for example.

function getGithubProfile(username) {
  return fetch(
    `https://api.github.com/users/${username}`
  ).then((res) => res.json());
}

We already know it's unpredictable because it breaks Rule #1. But beyond that, it's even more unpredictable because, given the same input, it will return an inconsistent output. Even if everything goes right, the promise we get back from getGithubProfile is different for each invocation.

const p1 = getGithubProfile("tylermcginnis")
const p2 = getGithubProfile("tylermcginnis")


p1 === p2 // false

And worse, if something does go wrong, there are a variety of other things that aren't the user's profile that might get returned.

So now we have our second rule.

Rule #2

Consistent Outputs

The Benefits

It's obvious that writing functions that follow our rules will make them more predictable, and therefore, less susceptible to bugs. However, in doing so, there are some other non-obvious benefits as well.

Composable

Because these functions don't depend on the context of the application and instead receive their inputs via explicit arguments, they're entirely program agnostic and therefore, extremely composable and reusable.

You can take these functions and compose them into or reuse them in any application, and they'll Just Work™.

Cacheable

Because these functions always return the same output given a certain input, the results can be cached. There's no use in calling these functions more than once given the same input, since the output will always be the same.

Say we had an expensive isPrime function that calculated if a given number (n) was a prime number.

const isPrime = (n) => {
  if (n === 1) {
    return false
  }


  for (let i = 2; i <= Math.sqrt(n); i++) {
    if (n % i === 0) {
      return false
    }
  }


  return true
}

This function is entirely predictable. It doesn't contain any side effects and given the same input, it'll always return the same output.

In this scenario, it doesn't make sense to invoke isPrime more than once for a given number. isPrime(997) is always going to return true no matter how many times we run it. Because it's predictable, we can cache and reuse the result.

let primeCache = {
  1: false
}


const isPrime = (n) => {
  if (typeof primeCache[n] === 'boolean') {
    return primeCache[n]
  }


  for (let i = 2; i <= Math.sqrt(n); i++) {
    if (n % i === 0) {
      primeCache[n] = false
      return false
    }
  }


  primeCache[n] = true
  return true
}


export default isPrime
import isPrime from "./isPrime"


isPrime(997) // true
isPrime(997) // true (from cache)
isPrime(997) // true (from cache)
🤨

Wait a minute...

For the observant reader, you may have noticed that isPrime is now technically an impure function since it depends on the context of the application – specifically, primeCache. This is a good example of how, in the real world, you can't always write pure functions.

With that said, notice that we've encapsulated isPrime and primeCache into their own module. The only way to modify primeCache is via isPrime and no other function in our application has access to or depends on primeCache.

Even though isPrime is technically impure, it's about the purest impure function we can write and I'd still consider it (pragmatically) predictable.

Testable

Because these functions are free of side effects and don't depend on the context of the application, they're optimized for testing. You can simply test that, given a certain input, the output is consistent with what you'd expect.

Readable

Because these functions don't depend on the context of the application and are free of side effects, they're optimized for readability. The inputs are explicit, the outputs are explicit, and there are no surprises in between.

🌎

Pragmatic Predictability

In an ideal world, we'd always write entirely predictable functions. The problem, of course, is we don't live in an ideal world. Here's how I think about maximizing predictability in an unpredictable world.

Whenever you have to write code that breaks one of our two rules, abstract it to its own function, then create clear rules for interacting with that function (similar to what we did earlier with our isPrime function).

Network requests are the perfect example of this. At face value, network requests are simple. You request some resource, wait a little bit, then you get it. In reality, it's not so simple. Race conditions, slow networks, outages, and opaque errors are just a few of the possible things that can go wrong.

However, if you're able to create a clear abstraction over that complexity, you can minimize the possible complications that it can have on your application as a whole.

The Big Reveal

If it's not already obvious, this idea isn't a new concept that we've invented. Instead, we've essentially re-created the functional programming principle of Pure Functions. A function is considered pure if it contains no side effects and if, given the same input, it always returns the same output.

By embracing and striving to keep your functions pure, you'll naturally increase the predictability of your program while minimizing the amount of bugs it has.

So what does this have to do with React? Let's find out.

You can take these functions and compose them into or reuse them in any application, and they'll Just Work™.

Paul
3 days ago

Analogy for pure function: Taco Vending Machine

Deterministic: Yes, just like a taco vending machine, a pure function is deterministic. If you select a cheese taco and put in the right amount of money, you'll always get a cheese taco. Similarly, a pure function with the same input will always produce the same output.

No Side Effects: The machine's operation of dispensing a taco does not affect anything outside of the machine. It doesn't change the state of the room it's in, nor does it have any effect on other machines. This is like a pure function not altering any external state or data.

Consistency: Every time you put in the correct money and select your taco, you get exactly what you asked for. This is like a pure function always giving the same output for the same inputs.

Predictability: Yes, the machine is predictable because it always dispenses the type of taco selected for the given input (money and taco type). Similarly, a pure function’s behavior is predictable.

Reusability: The concept of reusability is slightly different in the context of functions. It means that the function can be used in different contexts or parts of the program without being modified. For the vending machine, it would be like being able to dispense tacos in any location or setting without needing to change how it works.

Easy to Debug: If the machine stops dispensing tacos, it's easy to check the specific parts: is there money? Are there enough ingredients? This is like a pure function where, if there’s an issue, you only need to look at the function itself and its immediate inputs.

Paul
3 days ago

Analogy for impure functions: Taco Stand

Non-Deterministic: Each time you order a taco, the outcome might be slightly different. Perhaps the chef adds a bit more cheese or spice, or the ingredients vary slightly based on what's available that day. Similarly, an impure function might return different results with the same inputs, depending on external factors.

Side Effects: Ordering a taco at the stand might have several side effects: The chef might shout your order out loud, affecting the environment (akin to modifying a global state). Ingredients get used up, changing the stand's inventory (altering external state). Maybe each taco order is recorded in a log book (similar to writing to a database).

Variable Outputs: The taco you receive can vary depending on external conditions like the time of day, the chef's mood, or the available ingredients. This variability is like an impure function whose output can change even with the same specified inputs.

Interacting with the World: The taco stand is part of a larger ecosystem. It interacts with customers, suppliers, and even weather conditions. This interaction is akin to impure functions which often deal with real-world complexities like API calls, user inputs, or global state management.

Difficulty in Predictability and Debugging: If something goes wrong (say, the tacos aren't as expected), finding the cause is more challenging. Was it the ingredients, the chef's technique, or something else? This mirrors the complexity of debugging impure functions, which might depend on many external and changing factors.
